subject: Chemistry
q:
On complete combustion 1.0 g of an organic compound (X) gave 1.46 g of \(\mathrm{CO}_2\) and 0.567 g of \(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\). The empirical formula mass of compound \((\mathrm{X})\) is ________ g.
(Given molar mass in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{O}: 16\))
question:
On complete combustion 1.0 g of an organic compound (X) gave 1.46 g of \(\mathrm{CO}_2\) and 0.567 g of \(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\). The empirical formula mass of compound \((\mathrm{X})\) is ________ g.
(Given molar mass in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \mathrm{C}: 12, \mathrm{H}: 1, \mathrm{O}: 16\))
solution:
Moles of ' C ' \(=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{CO}_2}=\frac{1.46}{44}=0.033\)
Moles of ' C ' \(=\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{C}}=0.033 \times 12\)
Moles of 'H' \(=2 \times \mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}=2 \times \frac{0.567}{18}=0.063\)
Mass of ' H ' \(=0.0063\)
Mass of Oxygen \((\mathrm{O})=1-\left(\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{c}}+\mathrm{W}_{\mathrm{H}}\right)\) \(=1-(0.033 \times 12+0.063 \times 1)=0.541 \mathrm{gm}\)
Moles of ' O ' \(=\frac{0.541}{16}=0.033\)
Empirical formula \(=\mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{O}\)
Empirical formula mass \(=30\).

subject: Chemistry
q:
The density of $3 \mathrm{M}$ solution of sodium chloride is $1.252 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$. The molality of the solution will be :
$\left(\right.$ molar mass, $\left.\mathrm{NaCl}=585 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
question:
The density of $3 \mathrm{M}$ solution of sodium chloride is $1.252 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}$. The molality of the solution will be :
$\left(\right.$ molar mass, $\left.\mathrm{NaCl}=585 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
solution:
The relation between molarity (M) and molality $(m)$ is
$d=\mathrm{M}\left(\frac{1}{m}+\frac{\mathrm{M}_2}{1000}\right), \mathrm{M}_2=$ Mol. mass of solute
On putting value
$$
1.252=3\left(\frac{1}{m}+\frac{58.5}{1000}\right)
$$
on solving $m=2.79$

subject: Chemistry
q: An organic compound has \(42.1 \%\) carbon, \(6.4 \%\) hydrogen and remainder is oxygen. If its molecular weight is 342, then its molecular formula is :
question: An organic compound has \(42.1 \%\) carbon, \(6.4 \%\) hydrogen and remainder is oxygen. If its molecular weight is 342, then its molecular formula is :
solution: only $\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}$ has $42.1 \%$ carbon, $6.4 \%$ hydrogen $\& 51.5$ percent oxygen.

subject: Chemistry
q: Amongst the following statements, that which was not proposed by Dalton was :
question: Amongst the following statements, that which was not proposed by Dalton was :
options: chemical reactions involve reorganization of atoms. These are neither created nor destroyed in a chemical reaction.
all the atoms of a given element have identical properties including identical mass. Atoms of different elements differ in mass.
when gases combine or reproduced in a chemical reaction they do so in a simple ratio by volume provided all gases are at the same T & P.
solution: When gases combine or reproduced in a chemical reaction they do so in a simple ratio by volume provided all gases are at the same T & P.

subject: Chemistry
q: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>681</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>7</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>21</mn></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> (Nearest Integer)
question: The number of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> atoms in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>681</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>7</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>5</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mn>3</mn></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>6</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>21</mn></msup></math>. The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">A</mi></msub><mo>=</mo><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>02</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>23</mn></msup><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>mol</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math> (Nearest Integer)
solution:
Given mass of compounds =681 g

Molar mass of C7H5N3O6=12×7+5×1+14×3+6×16

=84+5+42+96

=227

Number of moles of compound =681227=3 moles

Number of moles of N=3×number of moles of compound

=3×3=9 moles

Number of atoms of N =9×6.02×1023

=5418×1021

So x=5418

subject: Chemistry
q:
Combustion of glucose \(\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6\right)\) produces \(\mathrm{CO}_2\) and water. The amount of oxygen (in g) required for the complete combustion of \(900 \mathrm{~g}\) of glucose is :
[Molar mass of glucose in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}=180\) ]
question:
Combustion of glucose \(\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6\right)\) produces \(\mathrm{CO}_2\) and water. The amount of oxygen (in g) required for the complete combustion of \(900 \mathrm{~g}\) of glucose is :
[Molar mass of glucose in \(\mathrm{g} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}=180\) ]
solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_{6(\mathrm{~s})}+6 \mathrm{O}_{2(\mathrm{~g})} \longrightarrow 6 \mathrm{CO}_{2(\mathrm{~g})}+6 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_{(\theta)} \\ & \frac{900}{180} \\ & =5 \mathrm{~mol} \quad 30 \mathrm{~mol}\end{aligned}$
Mass of $\mathrm{O}_2$ required $=30 \times 32=960 \mathrm{gm}$

q: The ammonia <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> released on quantitative reaction of <math><mn>0.6 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> urea <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> with sodium hydroxide <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> can be neutralized by
question: The ammonia <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>3</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> released on quantitative reaction of <math><mn>0.6 </mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> urea <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><msub><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> with sodium hydroxide <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi></mrow></mfenced></math> can be neutralized by
options: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>200</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mn>00</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.2</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math>

<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0.1</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></math>
solution:
NH2CONH2 + 2NaOH →2NH3 +Na2CO3

By, stoichiometry, 1 mol of urea gives 2 mol NH3

Moles of urea ==0.6 gm60 gm/mol=0.01 mol

So, 0.01 mole urea produce 0.02 mole NH3

To neutralize ammonia HCl is required

NH3 +HCl→ NH4Cl

By equation, it is clear that 1 mol of ammonia is neutralized by 1mol of HCl.

So, 0.02 mol of ammonia neutralized by 0.02 mol of HCl

∴Mole of HCl=0.02 mole

Equivalent of HCl=normality×volume=0.2 N×100 mL=0.02 equivalent

As n-factor of HCl is 1, so number of equivalents=number of moles=0.02 moles

Hence, option 3 is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
In the reaction,
$2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s})+6 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
question:
In the reaction,
$2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{s})+6 \mathrm{HCl}(\mathrm{aq}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Al}^{3+}(\mathrm{aq})+6 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}(\mathrm{aq})+3 \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$
options: $11.2 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ at STP is produced for every mole of HCl consumed.
$33.6 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ is produced regardless of temperature and pressure for every mole of Al that reacts.
$67.2 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{H}_{2}(\mathrm{~g})$ at STP is produced for every mole of Al that reacts.
solution:
Reaction: $2\text{Al}(s) + 6\text{HCl}(aq) \rightarrow 2\text{Al}^{3+}(aq) + 6\text{Cl}^-(aq) + 3\text{H}_2(g)$
Checking each option:
(1) Per 1 mole HCl: From stoichiometry, 6 mol HCl produces 3 mol H₂, so 1 mol HCl produces 0.5 mol H₂ = 11.2 L at STP. TRUE
(2) Per 1 mole Al: 2 mol Al produces 3 mol H₂, so 1 mol Al produces 1.5 mol H₂ = 33.6 L at STP only, not regardless of T and P. FALSE
(3) Volume ratio at same T,P: 6 mol HCl : 3 mol H₂ = 2:1, so 12 L HCl : 6 L H₂. TRUE
(4) Per 1 mole Al: 1.5 mol H₂ = 33.6 L, not 67.2 L. FALSE
Option (1) is correct.

subject: Chemistry
q:
0.27 g of a long chain fatty acid was dissolved in 100 cm3 of hexane. 10 mL  of this solution was added dropwise to the surface of water in a round watch glass. Hexane evaporates and a monolayer is formed. The distance from edge to centre of the watch glass is 10 cm . What is the height of the monolayer?
[Density of fatty acid =0.9 g cm-3;π=3]
question:
0.27 g of a long chain fatty acid was dissolved in 100 cm3 of hexane. 10 mL  of this solution was added dropwise to the surface of water in a round watch glass. Hexane evaporates and a monolayer is formed. The distance from edge to centre of the watch glass is 10 cm . What is the height of the monolayer?
[Density of fatty acid =0.9 g cm-3;π=3]
solution:
Mass of fatty acid =0.027 g in 10 ml solution

Density of fatty acid 0.9  g/cc

Volume of fatty acid =0.0270.9=0.03 cc

Area of plate =πr2=3×102=300  cm2

Height of fatty acid layer =volumearea=0.03300=10-4 cm = 10-6m

subject: Chemistry
q: Volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>NaOH</mi></math> (formula weight <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ) which can be prepared from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>84</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>dm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>.
question: Volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mo> </mo><mi>NaOH</mi></math> (formula weight <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi>mol</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> ) which can be prepared from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>84</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>NaOH</mi></math> is ____<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><msup><mi>dm</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math>.
solution:
Number of moles = Mass / Molar mass​

Molar mass of NaOH=40 g/mol

By Using  the definition of molarity to find the volume of the solution:

Molarity= Number of moles of soluteVolume of solution (in liters)

M=nNaOHVsol( in L)⇒3=(84/40)V⇒V=0.7 L=7×10-1 L

subject: Chemistry
q: Number of moles and number of molecules in $1.4187$ L of $SO_2$ at STP respectively are:
question: Number of moles and number of molecules in $1.4187$ L of $SO_2$ at STP respectively are:
solution:
Volume of $SO_2$ at STP $= 1.4187$ L

Molar volume of an ideal gas at STP is approximately $22.414$ L.

Number of moles $n = \dfrac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Molar Volume}} = \dfrac{1.4187}{22.414} = 0.0633$

Number of molecules $= n \times N_A$

Number of molecules $= 0.0633 \times 6.022 \times 10^{23} = 3.812 \times 10^{22}$

Answer: $0.0633; 3.812 \times 10^{22}$

subject: Chemistry
q: The formula of a gaseous hydrocarbon which requires 6 times of its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> for complete oxidation and produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> is ________.
question: The formula of a gaseous hydrocarbon which requires 6 times of its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> for complete oxidation and produces <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn></math> times its own volume of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><mo>.</mo></math> The value of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></math> is ________.
solution:
Combustion reaction.

CxHy(g)+x+y4O2g→xCO2g+y2H2Oℓ

V               6 V             --                -                        Vx=4V

⇒x=4

Since: I Vo2=6×VCxHy

⇒Vx+y4=6 V

⇒x+y4=6⇒4+y4=6

⇒y=8

subject: Chemistry
q: Complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an oxygen containing compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>64</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>08</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The percentage of oxygen in the organic compound is :
question: Complete combustion of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>80</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of an oxygen containing compound <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi></msub><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi></msub></mrow></mfenced></math> gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>64</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>CO</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>08</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mo>.</mo></math> The percentage of oxygen in the organic compound is :
solution:
nCO2=2.6444=0.06

nc=0.06

weight of carbon =0.06×12=0.72 gm

nH2O=1.0818=0.06

nH=0.06×2=0.12

 weight of H=0.12 gm

= 1.8 - (0.72 + 0.12)

= 0.96 gram

 % weight of oxygen =0.961.8×100

The percentage of oxygen in the organic compound = 53.3%.

subject: Chemistry
q: 0.05 cm thick coating of silver is deposited on a plate of 0.05 m2 area. The number of silver atoms deposited on plate are _______×1023. (At mass Ag =108, d=7.9 g cm-3 Round off to the nearest integer.
question: 0.05 cm thick coating of silver is deposited on a plate of 0.05 m2 area. The number of silver atoms deposited on plate are _______×1023. (At mass Ag =108, d=7.9 g cm-3 Round off to the nearest integer.
solution:
Volume of silver coating =0.05×0.05×10000=25 cm3

Given, Density =7.9 g cm-3

Density = Mass/ Volume 

Mass of silver deposited =25×7.9 g

Moles of silver atoms = mass / molar mass =25×7.9108

Number of silver atoms =25×7.9108×6.023×1023

=11.01×1023

Ans. 11

subject: Chemistry
q: $25 \mathrm{ml}$ of a solution of barrium hydroxide on titration with a $0.1$ molar solution of hydrochloric acid gave a litre value of $35 \mathrm{ml}$. The molarity of barium hydroxide solution was
question: $25 \mathrm{ml}$ of a solution of barrium hydroxide on titration with a $0.1$ molar solution of hydrochloric acid gave a litre value of $35 \mathrm{ml}$. The molarity of barium hydroxide solution was
solution:
$\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2+2 \mathrm{HCI} \rightarrow \mathrm{BaCl}_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Applying Molarity equation, $\frac{\mathrm{M}_1 \mathrm{~V}_1}{\frac{1}{\left(\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2\right)}}=\frac{\mathrm{M}_2 \mathrm{~V}_2}{2}$ or $25 \times \mathrm{M}_1=\frac{0.1 \times 35}{2} \therefore \mathrm{M}_1=\frac{0.1 \times 35}{2 \times 25}=\frac{0.7}{10}=0.07$

subject: Chemistry
q: The ratio of masses of oxygen and nitrogen in a particular gaseous mixture is 1 : 4. The ratio of number of their molecules is:
question: The ratio of masses of oxygen and nitrogen in a particular gaseous mixture is 1 : 4. The ratio of number of their molecules is:
solution:
Let the mass of oxygen be x gm
∴   Mass of nitrogen = 4x gm
Number of molecules = 6.023 x 1023 x number of moles
∴   Ratio = 6.023 × 1 0 2 3 × no. of moles of oxygen 6.023 × 1 0 2 3 × no. of moles of nitrogen
              = m O 2 M O 2 m N 2 M N 2 = x 3 2 4 x 2 8
              = 7 3 2 = 7 : 32

q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> mole of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>PbS</mi></math> is oxidised by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> to get <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mo>=</mo></math>
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn></math> mole of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>PbS</mi></math> is oxidised by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>3</mn></msub></math> to get <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi></math> moles of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">O</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>.</mo></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">Y</mi><mo>=</mo></math>
solution:
Given that, 1 mole of PbS is oxidised by X moles of O3 to get Y moles of O2.

The balanced chemical equation can be written as,

PbS+4O3→PbSO4+4O2

Here x = 4, y = 4 

Hence,

 X+Y=4+4=8

If <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math> is the density of the solution in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mi>gmI</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is the molarity and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> is the mole fraction of the <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>2</mn><mtext>nd </mtext></msup></math> component, then <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> can be expressed as :
solution: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>1000</mn><msub><mi>dx</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></mrow><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub><mo>+</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mfenced><mrow><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>2</mn></msub><mo>-</mo><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></mrow></mfenced></mrow></mfrac></math>

q: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>84</mn><mi>mg</mi></math> sample of polyhydric alcoholic compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' of molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>92</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math> gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>344</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> gas at STP. The number of alcoholic hydrogen present in compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' is
question: A <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>84</mn><mi>mg</mi></math> sample of polyhydric alcoholic compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' of molar mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>92</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>mol</mi></math> gave <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>344</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mL</mi></math> of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi mathvariant="normal">H</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> gas at STP. The number of alcoholic hydrogen present in compound '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">X</mi></math>' is
solution:
Number of moles of alcoholic compound =Given massMolar mass = 1.84×10-392.0 = 2×10-5 moles

Number of moles of hydrogen gas produced = Volume of gas at STP22400 ml = 1.34422400 = 6×10-5 moles

Monohydric alcohol will react with active metal to give 12 mole of hydrogen gas.

Since per mole of given alcohol is giving 3 moles of hydrogen gas.

So number of alcoholic hydrogen present in compound should be =6.

subject: Chemistry
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>56</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of nitrogen gas is mixed with excess of hydrogen gas and it is found that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of ammonia gas is produced, The volume of unused nitrogen gas is found to be_____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math>.
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>56</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of nitrogen gas is mixed with excess of hydrogen gas and it is found that <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math> of ammonia gas is produced, The volume of unused nitrogen gas is found to be_____ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">L</mi></math>.
solution: Gay-Lussac's Law proposes a relationship between the combining volumes of gases with respect to the reactants and gaseous products. According to this law, when gases react together to produce gaseous products, the volumes of reactants and products bear a simple whole number ratio with each other, provided volumes are measured at same temperature and pressure.

subject: Chemistry
q: 80 mL of a hydrocarbon on mixing with 264 mL of oxygen in a closed U-tube undergoes complete combustion. The residual gases after cooling to 273 K occupy 224 mL. When the system is treated with KOH solution, the volume decreases to 64 mL. The formula of the hydrocarbon is :
question: 80 mL of a hydrocarbon on mixing with 264 mL of oxygen in a closed U-tube undergoes complete combustion. The residual gases after cooling to 273 K occupy 224 mL. When the system is treated with KOH solution, the volume decreases to 64 mL. The formula of the hydrocarbon is :
solution:
KOH absorbs $CO_2$. Volume of $CO_2$ produced = $224 - 64 = 160$ mL
Unreacted $O_2 = 64$ mL, so $O_2$ consumed = $264 - 64 = 200$ mL
For hydrocarbon $C_xH_y$: $\frac{CO_2}{\text{Hydrocarbon}} = \frac{160}{80} = 2 \Rightarrow x = 2$
$O_2$ consumed per mole = $x + \frac{y}{4} = \frac{200}{80} = 2.5$
$2 + \frac{y}{4} = 2.5 \Rightarrow y = 2$
Hydrocarbon is $C_2H_2$

subject: Chemistry
q:
Butane reacts with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water following the equation given below
$\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_{10}(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
If 174.0 kg of butane is mixed with 320.0 kg of $\mathrm{O}_2$, the volume of water formed in litres is ________.(Nearest integer)
[Given : (a) Molar mass of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{O}$ are 12, 1, $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively, (b) Density of water $\left.=1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\right]$
question:
Butane reacts with oxygen to produce carbon dioxide and water following the equation given below
$\mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_{10}(\mathrm{~g})+\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{l})$
If 174.0 kg of butane is mixed with 320.0 kg of $\mathrm{O}_2$, the volume of water formed in litres is ________.(Nearest integer)
[Given : (a) Molar mass of $\mathrm{C}, \mathrm{H}, \mathrm{O}$ are 12, 1, $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ respectively, (b) Density of water $\left.=1 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~mL}^{-1}\right]$
solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{C}_4 \mathrm{H}_{10}+\frac{13}{2} \mathrm{O}_2 \rightarrow 4 \mathrm{CO}_2+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \\
& 3 \times 10^3 \quad 10 \times 10^3
\end{aligned}$
Moles of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ formed $=\mathrm{n}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}=5 \times \frac{2}{13} \times 10 \times 10^3$
Then $\mathrm{w}_{\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}=\frac{10^5}{13} \times 18$
$=1.3846 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~g}$
Volume of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ will be $=138.46$ litre.
Ans. 138

subject: Chemistry
q:
Choose the correct statements.
(A) Weight of a substance is the amount of matter present in it.
(B) Mass is the force exerted by gravity on an object.
(C) Volume is the amount of space occupied by a substance.
(D) Temperatures below \(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) are possible in Celsius scale, but in Kelvin scale negative temperature is not possible.
(E) Precision refers to the closeness of various measurements for the same quantity.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
question:
Choose the correct statements.
(A) Weight of a substance is the amount of matter present in it.
(B) Mass is the force exerted by gravity on an object.
(C) Volume is the amount of space occupied by a substance.
(D) Temperatures below \(0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\) are possible in Celsius scale, but in Kelvin scale negative temperature is not possible.
(E) Precision refers to the closeness of various measurements for the same quantity.
Choose the correct answer from the options given below :
solution: Mass of substance is amount of matter present in it. Weight is force exerted by gravity on object.
